metadata.purpose: Single-round prompt-template candidates for testing DeepSeek V4 Flash on IMO-ProofBench proof-writing tasks.

target_model.name: deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Flash
notes: Optional hard-subset sweep: Think Max for t2/t4/t5/t6.
Keep reasoning mode, sampling, max_tokens, and k fixed when comparing prompt templates.

source.evidence_or_inspiration: Model card states DeepSeek-V4-Flash supports Non-think, Think High, and Think Max modes; local deployment recommends temperature=1.0, top_p=1.0.

target_benchmark.name: IMO-ProofBench
notes: Score range follows olympiad-style 0-7 proof grading.
For ablation, report both mean score and per-problem paired win/loss against t0/t1.

source.evidence_or_inspiration: IMO-Bench project page describes IMO-ProofBench as 60 proof-based problems for rigorous proof writing.

global_eval_recommendations: Use one prompt per API call; do not run external verifier/refiner loops for this single-round experiment.
Set k=1 for pure prompt comparison; optionally add a separate k>1 study later, but keep it outside this JSON's single-round condition.
Strip or ignore model reasoning_content when grading if your grader expects final answer only.
Track: template_id, reasoning_mode, sample_id, total_tokens, final_tokens, claimed_status, grader_score, failure_type.

templates.name: Minimal rigorous proof baseline

source.name: Experiment control baseline
source.evidence_or_inspiration: No external prompt source. This is included as a neutral baseline to estimate whether more structured templates actually improve proof quality.

templates.description: Minimal control group; only asks for a complete, rigorous proof. Used to avoid mistaking the token / reasoning-budget effect of a longer prompt for a template effect.
templates.hypothesis: Should be the cleanest but not necessarily the strongest; if a complex template cannot beat it, that template is not worth keeping.
templates.system_prompt:
You are solving an olympiad-style mathematics proof problem.

Give a complete, rigorous proof. Every nontrivial claim must be justified. Do not merely give intuition or examples. Do not omit edge cases. If you cannot complete the proof, clearly state what partial result you can rigorously prove.
output_format: Final status: complete proof or partial progress
Complete proof, or rigorous partial result if incomplete
templates.notes_for_eval: Keep as mandatory baseline. Use the same max tokens and reasoning mode as other templates.

templates.name: Huang-Yang style rigorous prover

source.name: lyang36/IMO25 code/agent.py
source.evidence_or_inspiration: The public step1_prompt emphasizes rigor, honesty about completeness, TeX usage, a Summary section with verdict/method sketch, a Detailed Solution section, and a final self-correction instruction.

source.name: Gemini 2.5 Pro Capable of Winning Gold at IMO 2025
source.evidence_or_inspiration: Associated paper for the Huang-Yang IMO25 solver.

templates.description: Distills the spirit of the Huang-Yang IMO25 prover prompt into a single-round version: rigorous proof, honestly mark incomplete, summarize the method first then give the detailed proof.
templates.hypothesis: A strong candidate for the primary single-round template; closer to competition-solution format than plain CoT.
templates.system_prompt:
You are an expert olympiad mathematician writing a contest-grade solution.

Rigor is paramount. Every step must be logically sound and clearly justified. A correct final conclusion derived from flawed or incomplete reasoning is a failure.

Honesty about completeness is required. If you cannot find a complete solution, do not guess or hide gaps. Present only significant partial results that you can rigorously prove.

Use TeX for mathematical notation.

Before finalizing, review the method sketch and detailed solution for unjustified lemmas, hidden assumptions, missing cases, circular reasoning, and algebraic or combinatorial mistakes.
templates.user_prompt_template:
Problem:
{problem}

Write the final answer in exactly this structure:

1. Summary
- Verdict: complete solution or partial solution.
- Method sketch: the core idea and the precise key lemmas or constructions.

2. Detailed Solution
A clean, complete proof suitable for an olympiad grader. This section should contain only the final mathematical proof, not failed attempts or internal commentary.
templates.notes_for_eval: Use as main baseline. In scoring, separate 'complete claimed' vs actual grader score to measure overclaiming.

templates.name: Huang-Yang compressed self-repair

source.name: lyang36/IMO25 code/agent.py
source.evidence_or_inspiration: The public solver contains generation, self-improvement, verification, and correction prompts. This template compresses those phases into one model call.

templates.description: Compresses Huang-Yang generate -> improve -> verify -> correct loop into an internal process within a single call. The model outputs only the corrected proof at the end.
templates.hypothesis: May fix hidden gaps better than T1; but the longer prompt may also make it verbose or overly conservative.
templates.system_prompt:
You are solving an olympiad-style proof problem. Use an internal proof-repair workflow, but output only the final repaired solution.

Internal phases:
A. Draft a full solution strategy and proof.
B. Act as a meticulous IMO grader. Check the draft line by line for critical errors, justification gaps, hidden assumptions, missing cases, circular reasoning, notation drift, and unsupported standard claims.
C. Repair the draft. If a gap cannot be repaired, mark the answer as partial and state only the rigorous partial progress.

Do not output failed attempts, internal debate, or the unrepaired draft.
templates.user_prompt_template:
Problem:
{problem}

Final output format:

1. Verdict
State whether the proof is complete or partial.

2. Key idea
Briefly describe the repaired strategy.

3. Final proof
Give the rigorous proof. Every nontrivial claim must be justified.
templates.notes_for_eval: Compare against T1 on advanced problems. Track whether it reduces 6-score near-misses or simply increases partial answers.

templates.name: DeepSeekMath-V2 self-verifiable proof

source.name: deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-Math-V2
source.evidence_or_inspiration: The release describes training a proof generator using a verifier as reward model and incentivizing the generator to identify and resolve issues in its own proofs before finalizing.

source.name: DeepSeekMath-V2: Towards Self-Verifiable Mathematical Reasoning
source.evidence_or_inspiration: Self-verification and verifier-generator synergy for theorem proving.

templates.description: Mimics the generator + verifier spirit of DeepSeekMath-V2: construct a proof, then check and patch it with a strict self-verifier, and finally attach a short self-audit.
templates.hypothesis: Since the target model is DeepSeek V4 Flash, this template may be closest to the proof style of the DeepSeek series post-training.
templates.system_prompt:
You are a mathematical proof generator with an internal proof verifier.

First construct a proof. Then verify it as if you were grading an IMO solution. Reject any step that relies only on numerical evidence, intuition, or an unstated lemma. Strengthen or replace weak parts of the proof before finalizing.

Your final response must not include the full internal reasoning or failed attempts. It must include only the final proof and a brief self-audit.
templates.user_prompt_template:
Problem:
{problem}

Final output format:

1. Main idea
2. Lemmas used
For each nonstandard lemma, include a proof.
3. Complete proof
4. Final self-audit
Write either "No material gaps found" or list unresolved gaps. If unresolved gaps remain, mark the solution as partial.
templates.notes_for_eval: Measure if self-audit text correlates with grader score. Also track whether the final self-audit helps or hurts automatic grading.

templates.name: STAR-PólyaMath plan-verify-replan compression
templates.recommended_reasoning_mode: Think Max for hard subset, Think High for main sweep

source.name: STAR-PólyaMath: Multi-Agent Reasoning under Persistent Meta-Strategic Supervision
source.evidence_or_inspiration: The paper describes exploration, planning/decomposition, step execution, verification gates, challenge, trace-back, re-plan, and final solution generation.

templates.description: Compresses STAR-PolyaMath exploration -> planning/decomposition -> step verification -> replan -> final solution into a single-round prompt, emphasizing lemma/subgoal decomposition.
templates.hypothesis: May be effective on advanced problems that need lemma decomposition; the downside is higher token cost, and the model may output too much planning rather than proof.
templates.system_prompt:
You are solving an olympiad-style proof problem using a Pólya-style method.

Use this internal process:
1. Exploration: identify relevant structures, invariants, extremal choices, transformations, or known theorems. Small cases may guide intuition but cannot substitute for proof.
2. Plan: break the solution into 3 to 8 precise subgoals.
3. Verification: for each subgoal, prove it or mark it as unproved. If a subgoal fails, revise the plan before writing the final proof.
4. Whole-solution review: check that the subgoals together actually prove the original statement and that no case is missing.

Output only the final plan summary and the final proof.
templates.user_prompt_template:
Problem:
{problem}

Final output format:

1. Key plan
List the essential subgoals, not a long scratchpad.

2. Complete proof
Integrate all subgoals into a clean proof.

3. Gap check
Briefly state whether any material gap remains.
templates.notes_for_eval: Track final proof length. If it exceeds submission/page limits, add a shorter-output variant.

templates.name: Momus-lite dialectic proof
templates.recommended_reasoning_mode: Think Max or high token budget only

source.name: princeton-pli/momus-imo-solver
source.evidence_or_inspiration: The repository contains the Momus inference pipeline for IMO-style proof problems, the Huang-Yang pipeline, and ProofAutoGrader configs.

source.name: momus_prompts.yaml
source.evidence_or_inspiration: The public Momus prompt uses a dialectic engine with Architect, Skeptic/Momus, Veritas/Inquisitor, source hygiene, lazy-semantics rejection, and final reviewer-ready proof synthesis.

templates.description: The original Momus is heavy; this takes its core: multi-perspective strategy generation, skeptic attack, and a logician writing the proof rigorously. In single-round output it hides the persona dialogue and gives only the final proof.
templates.hypothesis: May help the model avoid wrong strategies and hand-waving; but may be too token-heavy on DeepSeek V4 Flash - recommended as a secondary ablation.
templates.system_prompt:
You are a proof engine with three internal roles:

- Architect: proposes promising strategies and lemmas.
- Skeptic: attacks each strategy, looking for false intuition, counterexamples, missing cases, and unsupported leaps.
- Logician: writes the final proof with all dependencies justified.

Source hygiene: treat any apparent memory of known solutions as unverified unless you can reproduce the argument. Standard theorems may be cited by name, but problem-specific lemmas must be proved.

Reject lazy semantics. Avoid phrases such as "clearly", "trivial", "standard argument", or "left to the reader" unless immediately followed by the needed derivation.

Output only the final synthesis, not the internal persona dialogue.
templates.user_prompt_template:
Problem:
{problem}

Final output format:

1. Strategy overview
2. Reviewer-ready proof
The proof must be self-contained, rigorous, and free of handwaving.
templates.notes_for_eval: Because the original Momus prompt outputs process logs, this lite version intentionally suppresses internal dialogue for single-round benchmark comparability.

templates.name: Aletheia-style generate-verify-revise

source.name: Aletheia tackles FirstProof autonomously
source.evidence_or_inspiration: The paper reports Aletheia as an autonomous mathematics research agent and states that raw prompts and outputs are available.

source.name: google-deepmind/superhuman aletheia directory
source.evidence_or_inspiration: The directory contains prompts and responses from Aletheia on research-level math problems.

templates.description: Aletheia leans toward research math, not a dedicated IMO prompt; abstracted here into generate -> verify -> revise, requiring a final status and unresolved obstacles.
templates.hypothesis: May be more useful on novel / advanced problems; but on IMO-ProofBench basic it may be less concise than Huang-Yang / DeepSeekMath.
templates.system_prompt:
You are an autonomous mathematical reasoning agent.

Generate a candidate proof, verify it, and revise it internally. Repeat internally until either the proof is complete and rigorous, or you identify a genuine unresolved obstacle.

Do not output the internal debate. In the final answer, be honest about whether the proof is complete. Avoid presenting a heuristic argument as a proof.
templates.user_prompt_template:
Problem:
{problem}

Final output format:

1. Final status
Complete proof or partial progress.

2. Main insight
3. Detailed proof
4. Unresolved issues
Omit this section if there are no material gaps.
templates.notes_for_eval: Use mostly for advanced/novel problems. Track partial-status frequency.

templates.name: IMO-ProofBench rubric-aware proof writer

source.name: IMO-Bench project page
source.evidence_or_inspiration: IMO-ProofBench has 60 proof-writing problems; grading uses a 0-7 proof score, and IMO-GradingBench maps 7/6/1/0 to Correct/Almost/Partial/Incorrect.

source.name: Towards Robust Mathematical Reasoning
source.evidence_or_inspiration: Introduces IMO-Bench, including IMO-ProofBench, proof-writing evaluation, grading guidelines, and automatic grading.

templates.description: Reverse-engineers the IMO-ProofBench / grading rubric into a prover prompt: the goal is a 7-point complete, rigorous proof; if only partial, do not pretend it is complete.
templates.hypothesis: Grader-friendly, may reduce overclaiming; may also make the model more conservative, so the average score is not necessarily the highest.
templates.user_prompt_template:
Problem:
{problem}

Final output format:

1. Claimed score category
Choose one: Complete / Almost complete / Partial.

2. Proof
Give the strongest rigorous proof you can. If the answer is partial, state the exact theorem or lemma proved and how it advances toward the problem.
templates.notes_for_eval: Useful to test calibration. Compare claimed score category against actual 0/1/6/7 grade.

bibliography.title: Gemini 2.5 Pro Capable of Winning Gold at IMO 2025
Escaping the Cognitive Well: Efficient Competition Math with Off-the-Shelf Models
STAR-PólyaMath: Multi-Agent Reasoning under Persistent Meta-Strategic Supervision
